questions.question: In paragraph 8, Teacher Zhang mentions that Ji-li “participates in educational reform”. What inference can be made about his motivation for bringing this up?

Read the following passage from paragraph 10:

“I closed my eyes. I saw Dad standing on a stage, his head bowed, his name written in large black letters, and then crossed out in red ink, on a sign hanging from his neck. I saw myself standing in the middle of the stage, facing thousands of people, condemning Dad for his crimes, raising my fist to lead the chant, ‘Down with Jiang Xi-reng.’ I saw Dad looking at me hopelessly, tears on his face.”

What does this suggest about Ji-li's relationship with her father?

responses.content: Teacher zhang is saying they can help her in school more

questions.question: In paragraph 21, Jo Ann Robinson said the verdict was a “bombshell.” What does she mean by this?

responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'Words' does not show engagement with the text or the question. It is a minimal-effort answer that does not reference any content from the passage or attempt to answer the question about what civil rights leaders were looking for. There is no evidence of text-specific engagement, so it fits the 'limited-effort' category.

responses.content: E.D. Nixon and other blacks leaders wanted to take the entire bus segregation issue into federal court. They hoped to demonstrate that segregated buses were illegal under the U.S. Constitution. But first they needed the strongest possible case-the arrest of a black rider who was above reproach, a person of unassailable character and reputation who could withstand the closest scrutiny. Claudette Colvin, Nixon felt, was too young and immature, too prone to emotional outbursts, to serve as standard-bearer for a long and expensive constitutional test case. As Nixon pointed out, she had fought with police, she came from the poorer side of black Montgomery, and it was later rumored that she was pregnant. “I had to be sure I had somebody I could win with…to ask people to give us a half million dollars to fight discrimination on a bus line,” Nixon said later

responses.bucket_rationale: The most recent response, 'Against,' is a one-word answer that does not demonstrate engagement with the specific question or the text. It does not reference any content from the passage or show an attempt to answer the question about what civil rights leaders were looking for. This fits the criteria for 'limited-effort.'

The most recent response, 'Segregation,' is a one-word answer that does not address the question about what qualities civil rights leaders were looking for in a person to represent their case. It does not show text-specific engagement or attempt to answer the question, and it is not a clarifying question. Therefore, it fits the 'limited-effort' category.
